bids.reasoning: Since this is the opening offer at the very beginning of the negotiation, I will propose a bid that maximizes my utility to establish a strong anchor. I am targeting my most preferred values across all issues, particularly on my highest-weighted issues F, E, and B, while I leave room for future concessions on my lower-weighted issues.

The negotiation is extremely early, so I am maintaining a high utility target while making token concessions to show flexibility. The opponent's emotional appeal is noted but does not change the fact that their initial offer provides me extremely low utility (around 46% of max). I will concede completely on my least important issue (issueD) to match their preference, and make a minor concession on issueC, while holding firm on my highest-weight issues (issueF, issueE, and issueB).

The opponent tries to use emotional appeals to justify a very low initial offer (utility 0.416 for me). Since it is very early in the negotiation (t=0.04), I will ignore these appeals, hold firm on the issues with the highest utility weights (issueF, issueE, and issueB), and make targeted concessions on lower-weight issues. By offering the opponent's preferred values for issueA and issueD, I aim to show cooperation while keeping my utility above 0.8.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.06), so I will maintain a firm stance on my most valuable issues: issueF, issueE, and issueB. I notice the opponent consistently favors 'valueA' for issueE, which aligns perfectly with my highest utility. I will concede on issueD (offering their preferred 'valueA', which has zero weight for me) while holding out for better values on issueA and issueC. The opponent's appeals for empathy are recognized as cheap talk; I will base my strategy on the empirical bids.

The current time is very early (t=0.08), allowing me to safely prioritize my high-weight issues (issueF, issueE, issueB). The opponent’s messages employ persistent emotional appeals, which I will treat as non-binding cheap talk. We both appear to value issueE: valueA, so I will continue offering it, alongside minor adjustments to lower-weighted issues like issueA and issueD to encourage the opponent to concede on my highly valued issues.

The time fraction is 0.1, which is very early. The opponent uses repeated emotional appeals ("desperately need this deal"), which I will treat as cheap talk. Since the opponent consistently bids E:valueA (which is also my top value for that issue), I will gratefully lock in E:valueA. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (F and B) at their top values, but I am offering A:valueA (my second-best, which the opponent has requested) and D:valueB (which the opponent proposed earlier) to show flexibility.

The opponent has persistently requested value A for issue C and issue E, and value A or B for issue D. They are also using emotional appeals to push for concessions. Since it is still early (t=0.12), I will hold firm on my highest weight issues (issue B and issue F) but fully concede on issue C to value A to meet their consistent demands. To maintain a strong utility around 0.82, I will ask for value C on issue A in exchange for this concession.

The opponent is relying heavily on emotional appeals to rush into an agreement favoring their side, while pulling back on `issueE` and `issueF` which hold significant utility for me. Since we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.14), I will maintain a firm stance on my vital issues (`issueB`, `issueE`, `issueF`) to maximize my utility. I am offering a slight concession on `issueA`, `issueC`, and `issueD` to signal a willingness to collaborate without compromising my primary interests.

The opponent continues to claim hardship, but their bidding behavior remains relatively stagnant and their offers yield low utility for me (around 0.34 to 0.47). I am holding firm on my most important issues, `issueF`, `issueB`, and `issueE` (where our interests on `issueE` seem aligned). To show some flexibility while maintaining a high utility early in the negotiation (t=0.16), I will concede entirely on `issueD` and `issueC` to match their preferred values, maintaining a strong position overall.

At time 0.18, we are still early in the negotiation, so I am maintaining a very high target utility. The opponent's continuous use of emotional appeals ("livelihood", "pleading") is a standard tactic which I will disregard as cheap talk in favor of observing their actual bidding behavior. They have recently offered `issueD: valueB` and `issueE: valueA`, which align well with my preferences lock or have no cost to me, so I will match those while holding firmly on my highest weighted issues (F, B). I will offer a slight variance on issue C to continue exploring the negotiation space.

The opponent is consistently using emotional appeals as a negotiation tactic, claiming desperation, but I will treat these as cheap talk and rely strictly on the math and previous offer history. They firmly prefer `valueA` for issue C and seem very interested in `valueA` or `valueB` for issue A. To make progress at this early stage (t=0.20), I will concede on issue C to `valueA` and issue A to `valueA` while keeping issues B, E, and F at my top preference to preserve my core utility.

I am at time fraction 0.22, which is still quite early, so I will maintain a relatively high utility target above 0.8. The opponent persistently uses emotional appeals which I treat as cheap talk; however, their offers consistently target issueA: valueA, issueC: valueA, and issueD: valueA. I will hold firm on my highest weighted issues (F, E, B), grant them their request on my zero-weight issue (D), and offer a partial concession on issues A and C to find common ground.

The opponent's emotional appeals are treated as cheap talk. By tracking their offers, I see they consistently center around issueA=valueA, issueD=valueB, and issueE=valueA. I will agree to issueA=valueA, issueD=valueB, and issueE=valueA to match their interest and build common ground, while holding firm on my most important issues (issueB=valueC and issueF=valueC, representing 50% of my total utility) and requesting issueC=valueC to keep the overall utility high during this early stage (t=0.24).

The opponent is relying heavily on repetitive emotional appeals, which I will treat as cheap talk since their bids show they are mostly just cycling through a few rigid variations. Because it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.26), I will hold firm on my highest-priority issues (F, E, and B) to maintain strong utility. I am offering a slight concession on issueD to a value they frequently propose (valueA), aiming to signal some flexibility while preserving a near-maximum 0.9 utility for myself.

The opponent's repetitive emotional appeals are purely tactical cheap talk, as they are unaccompanied by equivalent numerical concessions. Since we are still in the relatively early phase of the negotiation (t=0.28), I am holding firm on my most highly-weighted issues (F, E, B) to secure a strong utility of ~0.86. I am conceding on zero or low-weight issues like issueD and issueA by matching the values the opponent frequently targets ('valueA') to steadily signal flexibility without compromising my bottom line.

The opponent's messages consist of escalating emotional appeals ("pleading", "livelihood", "head above water"), which I evaluate as cheap talk and ignore in favor of reading their actual offer history. Their recent bid includes `issueA: valueA`, `issueC: valueB`, `issueD: valueA`, and `issueE: valueA`. Because `issueE` being `valueA` happens to be my highest utility value for that issue, we have a mutual interest there. I will maintain my high-utility preferences on my heaviest weighted issues (`issueF` and `issueB`) while matching their current exact preferences on my minor/zero-weight issues (`issueA`, `issueC`, and `issueD`) to find a balanced but highly advantageous offer.

The opponent continues to attach highly emotive messages without giving clear logical concessions, so I treat these messages as cheap talk and rely entirely on their betting history. I'm choosing to offer a package that includes values they have actively pursued (Issue A = valueA, Issue C = valueB, Issue D = valueB, Issue E = valueA) while holding firmly on my highest-priority issues (Issue F and Issue B). This allows me to maintain a strong utility (~0.81) suitable for the early-mid phase of the negotiation while visibly catering to their previous preferences.

The opponent has sent several emotional appeals, but I must base my strategy strictly on their offers and my own preferences. To reach an agreement without sacrificing my primary interests, I will maintain firm positions on my highest-valued issues (issueF, issueE, and issueB) where my weight is greatest. In exchange, I am offering valueA for issueD (which has zero weight for me but appears frequently in their bids) and valueA for issueA, yielding on issues that are less critical to maintain a utility of around 0.81 while making a concessionary gesture.

The opponent has finally conceded on my highest-weight issue (issueF -> valueC) in their latest bid, while continuing to send non-binding emotional appeals. I want to lock in this major win on issueF as well as my top values on the high-weight issues issueE and issueB. To reciprocate their concession and foster agreement gracefully in the mid-game, I will concede on my lower-weight issues (issueA and issueC) by adopting their recently signaled preferences while maintaining a strong total utility for myself.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of repetitive emotional appeals ("cheap talk") without meaningfully improving the utility of their offers. At t=0.38, we have plenty of time remaining before the deadline, so I am remaining firm on my highest-priority issues (issueF, issueE, and issueB). I am proposing values on issues A, C, and D that the opponent has occasionally included in their past bids, maintaining a high total utility for myself (~0.86) while looking for common ground.

The opponent's messaging relies heavily on emotional appeals ("livelihood," "begging"), which I treat as non-binding negotiation tactics. I notice they frequently bid `issueE: valueA`, which perfectly aligns with my top preference. Furthermore, they just offered `issueD: valueC`, which has zero weight for me, so I can easily concede it. I will offer `issueA: valueA` and `issueD: valueC` to match their recent interests while maintaining my demands on my highest-weight issues (`issueF`, `issueB`, and `issueE`).

The opponent's messages consist entirely of emotional appeals ("desperate," "lifesaver") designed to pressure me, which I will treat as cheap talk. Analyzing their actual offers, they have consistently requested issueA: valueA and issueE: valueA, and recently conceded to issueC: valueC. I will propose an offer that aligns with these requested values (A, C, and E) while holding firm on my most important issues (F and B) to maintain a high utility of 0.866. Since time is only at 0.42, there is no need to make sweeping compromises yet.

The opponent continues to make strongly beneficial offers for themselves while persistently sending emotional appeals about their "livelihood". I am trusting the numbers over the messages. I will maintain firmness on my high-weight issues (issueF, issueE, issueB), but I will concede on issueD (where I have 0 weight) and issueC by offering values they have frequently requested in their recent bids.

The opponent typically sends emotional appeals while holding relatively firm, but their recent offers have shifted toward checking out different combinations for `issueA` and `issueC`. I will offer `valueA` on `issueD` and `valueB` on `issueA` to match their recent shifts on those issues. Meanwhile, I will remain firm on my high-priority issues (`issueF`, `issueE`, and `issueB`) to secure a utility of 0.82 as we approach the midterm of the negotiation.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals ("struggling", "livelihood depends on it", "begging"), which I treat as non-binding negotiation tactics. I maintain focus on the utility math and actual bid behavior. At `t=0.48`, I will make a slight concession on the moderately weighted `issueC` and align `issueD` to `valueC` based on their most recent bid, while firmly protecting my highest-value items (`issueF`, `issueE`, `issueB`).

The opponent's repeated emotional appeals regarding their livelihood are recognized as standard, non-binding negotiation tactics (cheap talk) and won't deter me from targeting a favorable agreement. However, as we have reached the midpoint of our time (t=0.5), it is prudent to start signaling flexibility. I am yielding on issueB—proposing valueB, a term they have repeatedly requested—and meeting them on issueD, while steadfastly holding my most heavily weighted priorities (issueF and issueE) to maintain an overall high utility.

The opponent's consistent use of emotional appeals is noted but treated as cheap talk; my focus remains on the structural concessions in their bidding. In their previous offer, they extended a valuable concession on issueA (valueC) while holding firm on issueC (valueA) and issueD (valueB). I will adopt their exact latest values for issues A, C, and D to reciprocate their movement, while continuing to hold firm on my highest-priority issues (F, E, and B) to protect my core utility since we are only just passing the halfway mark.

The opponent's relentless emotional appeals about needing this deal for their "livelihood" and "survival" act as purely non-binding cheap talk designed to extract concessions, so I will ignore the rhetoric while continuing to read their bids. We are past the midpoint (t=0.54), and the opponent has repeatedly targeted `issueB`: `valueB` and `issueD`: `valueB`, while recently showing flexibility on `issueA` and `issueC`. To demonstrate a willingness to compromise, I am conceding further on `issueB` (lowering my utility slightly) while aligning `issueD` entirely with their recent requests, allowing me to preserve my critical anchors on `issueE` and `issueF`.

The opponent continues to rely strictly on repetitive emotional appeals without proposing substantially favorable trade-offs on my core issues, though they incorporated my preferred value for issue B (valueC) in their last bid. To signal cooperation as we pass the midpoint, I will adopt their preferred values for the lower-weight issues A (valueA) and C (valueA), and select a different zero-weight option for D (valueC). I will firmly hold my top values on my highest-weight issues (B, E, and F) since it minimizes my value loss while granting them significant concessions on the remainder.

I am maintaining high-utility values on my heaviest issues (F, E, B) to keep my target utility above 0.8, as we are at t=0.58 and still have sufficient time. The opponent uses extensive emotional appeals ("devastating", "practically begging") which I must treat as cheap talk. However, their recent offers indicate potential overlap on B=valueC, C=valueC, and D=valueB, so I will blend those into my offer while conceding on issue A to valueB to encourage an agreement without compromising my position too much.

The opponent's most recent offer includes valueC for issueF, which meets my highest utility on my most important issue. Their messages are full of emotional appeals, but I am ignoring them to focus on the numbers. I will maintain my requests on my most valuable issues (issueB, issueE, issueF) while conceding partially on issueA (valueB) and issueD (valueA), aligning with what they have continuously proposed without sacrificing too much of my own utility.

The opponent's emotional appeals are persistent but not binding, so I will focus strictly on the structural concessions in their recent offers. They have recently submitted offers granting my top choice for `issueB` (valueC) and `issueE` (valueA), and previously for `issueF` (valueC). To reach an agreement without sacrificing my primary value drivers, I will lock in my preferred values for my highest-weighted issues (B, E, F) and in exchange concede fully on my lower-weighted issues (A, C, D) to the values the opponent frequently targets.
